burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

cross_connections.display_label: Husband Dr. Foote helped found Republican Party 1856
Husband Dr. Foote helped found Republican Party 1856

narrative_blocks.header: Husband helped organize Republican Party, 1856
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles Leavitt's second husband Dr. Elial Todd Foote — physician, judge, and New York State Assemblyman — was one of the figures who helped organize the Republican Party in 1856 at its founding convention. He was an anti-slavery activist throughout his career. Their April 27, 1841 marriage came after Amelia's widowhood from her first husband Rev. Charles Jenkins of Greenfield Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Yale President Ezra Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia's mother Emilia Stiles was a daughter of Ezra Stiles — the President of Yale College from 1778 to 1795 — making Amelia a granddaughter of one of New England's most important late-eighteenth-century theological and academic figures. Her father Hon. Jonathan Leavitt was judge and businessman of Greenfield Massachusetts, and son of Sarah Hooker, putting Amelia at the intersection of the Yale-presidential and Hooker-descent lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Sarah Hooker; born Greenfield 1799
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia Stiles Leavitt was a granddaughter of Sarah Hooker through her father Hon. Jonathan Leavitt — Sarah had died eight years before Amelia's 1799 birth, so they never met. Amelia herself was born December 6, 1799 in Greenfield Massachusetts and died November 26, 1867 in New Haven Connecticut, where Dr. Foote had practiced medicine.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Jamestown, New York (at his own request).

X00158.prominent_name_reason: Dr. Elial Todd Foote (1796-1877) — physician, jurist, politician, abolitionist, historian. First physician of Jamestown, New York (1815). New York State Assembly 1819-20, 1826-27. Judge Chautauqua County Court of Common Pleas 1818-23; County Judge of Chautauqua County 1824-1843 (20 years, reappointed by successive governors). Purchased land from Holland Land Company on which large part of Jamestown NY was built. Founded Chautauqua County Bank 1831 (nearest bank previously 90 miles away in Buffalo). Anti-slavery activist; Liberty ticket supporter 1844; helped organize Republican Party 1856. His antislavery papers preserved at Chautauqua County Historical Society, McClurg Museum. Canal Committee member during Erie Canal construction session 1819-20. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elial_T._Foote

sources.short: Foote Family Genealogy, Abram W. Foote

cross_connections.display_label: Helped organize the Republican Party at New Haven 1856. Married.

cross_connections.notes: Foote was a speaker at the 1856 Republican Party organizing meeting in New Haven CT. Ashmun presided over the 1860 Republican National Convention that nominated Lincoln. Two Hooker-line connections to the founding of the Republican Party.

notable.notable_blurb: Founding speaker at 1856 Republican Party meeting

narrative_blocks.header: Helped organize Republican Party at New Haven, 1856
narrative_blocks.body: At a New Haven meeting in early 1856 he spoke at the organizing session of the Republican Party for the presidential campaign. A witness later recorded: 'Dr Elial T Foote was one of the speakers, and added greatly to the enthusiasm of the meeting.'

narrative_blocks.header: Democrat 'when mewling in nurses' arms' line
narrative_blocks.body: On the slavery question he declared he was a Democrat 'when they were mewling and puking in their nurses' arms!' — placing his anti-slavery commitment in the early Republic before the modern Democratic Party existed. He ran on the abolitionist Liberty ticket in 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: First settled physician of Jamestown NY, 1815
narrative_blocks.body: Arriving in 1815, he became the first settled physician of Jamestown New York. He served thirteen years in the State Assembly with Erie Canal committee work, founded the Chautauqua County Bank in 1831, and presided as First Judge of Chautauqua County from 1818 to 1843.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried at Jamestown by his own request, 1877
narrative_blocks.body: He died at New Haven Connecticut on 17 November 1877 at age eighty-one. At his own request he was buried back at Jamestown New York — the upstate town he had served sixty-two years as physician, banker, judge, and legislator.

sources.short: Emerson Davis, Biographical Sketches of Congregational Pastors

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1758 minister at Walpole and Charlemont
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at Walpole, New Hampshire on May 27, 1761. After 1767 he served the Charlemont, Massachusetts pulpit until 1785.

narrative_blocks.header: Uncle of Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Jemima Leavitt married Capt. David Ellsworth, making him the uncle of Oliver Ellsworth — Founding Father, signer of the Constitution, and third Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court. The Ellsworth and Leavitt households were closely tied through Suffield, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Dragged a runaway enslaved woman home by rope
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 he was dismissed from his Walpole ministry after allegedly dragging a runaway enslaved woman home behind his horse by a rope tied to his saddle. He was installed at Charlemont, Massachusetts three years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Dismissed from Charlemont for his Loyalist sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: He was dismissed from his Charlemont, Massachusetts ministry in 1785 for Loyalist sympathies during the Revolution. He preached on alone in a schoolhouse and sued his former parishioners for back wages, winning roughly £700.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died from medicine her daughter gave her
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Sarah Hooker Leavitt died in October 1791 at age 49 'as a consequence of taking wrong medicine' given to her by their daughter Clarissa. The obituary called the loss sudden and unexpected.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandsons defended him in a 1904 book
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his grandsons John Hooker Leavitt and William Hooker Leavitt published a 1904 volume titled 'A Sketch of the Life and Character of Rev. Jonathan Leavitt.' They tried to portray their controversial ancestor as a misunderstood and complicated man.

education.notes: B.A. — Yale 1758. Tier-2 spouse of Sarah Hooker (H00302). Two brothers also Yale graduates. Nephew-by-marriage to Chief Justice Oliver Ellsworth.

cross_connections.display_label: Her descendant designed USS Hartford's steam engine
Her grandson sheltered fugitive Basil Dorsey five years

bio.notes: Sarah No. 2 — replacement naming after first Sarah (H00301) who died April 1741

narrative_blocks.header: Married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt of Heath
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Jonathan Leavitt and moved to Heath, Massachusetts. Leavitt was twice dismissed from churches — once for dragging a runaway enslaved woman home by rope, and once for Loyalist sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children — eleven sons and one daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She had twelve children with Jonathan Leavitt — eleven sons and one daughter named Clarissa. EH lists only three; other sources confirm the full count of twelve.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter gave her wrong medicine, killing her
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly in 1791 after her daughter Clarissa gave her the wrong medicine. Clarissa herself died unmarried seven years later — never recovering from the accident.

narrative_blocks.header: Underground Railroad station owners descended from her
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her grandsons — Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt — operated Underground Railroad stations in Charlemont, Massachusetts, sheltering the fugitive Basil Dorsey for five years. Roger's home is on the National Park Service Underground Railroad Network to Freedom.

narrative_blocks.header: A wrong medicine, given by her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: She died suddenly at Heath on October 11, 1791, after taking the wrong medicine — handed to her, the Yale record says, by her daughter Clarissa. The funeral text was “He hath done all things well”; the Connecticut Courant carried the notice.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.
